sentences.text: Right.
Yes.
He went in there.
And he told him where I am.
And [?
Abba Weinstein ?] found out.
And he start to seek to get me out.
So, well, we found out, I was charged with having [INAUDIBLE] illegal papers, trying to smuggle people, [? this. ?] And it was a-- a big crime, like a court martial, or whatever you call it.
We had this man, this captain, he was the American captain, Captain Novinsky.
And he was real-- very nice, and very helpful.
And he was the best one we ever had.
Captain Novinsky came down, and he was like my lawyer.
And he defended me.
There was a court in there, and actually they brought me in.
And the end was, I went free.
Nothing-- nothing happened.
That was--
Who?
Leon--
Leon [?
Fisher. ?] He was-- yeah.
He was soldier.
He was one of the Americans.
He was the-- he was also one of them.
We had also Chaplain [?
Kohn. ?] They were with them.
Leon [?
Fisher ?] was-- he was the American [? commandant, ?] also.
There was a lot of American people, American soldiers and officers.
There was a Lieutenant [?
Ziburg. ?] And there was a lot of them [? there. ?] Talking about this Lieutenant [?
Fisher ?] and-- and Chaplain [?
Kohn, ?] I gotta to tell you one episode what happened.
One time, we had to send people-- we were gonna bring people from Salzburg to [INAUDIBLE].
We decided it was cheaper, was better, faster, to bring them people-- because before, we used to transport 'em in trucks.
So we-- we wanted to see how it's gonna go by train.
Because by train, we could put more people in there.
So, OK.
So we decided we're gonna put, like, 50 people in a train and send them over to [INAUDIBLE].
OK.
So they put 50 people in a train to go to [INAUDIBLE].
And sure enough-- I wasn't on that train.
Somebody else took these people.
Sure enough, they came to [INAUDIBLE], and they turned them back.
They came back to Salzburg.
OK.
So we're talking to [?
Abba Weinstein. ?] He says, look.
They brought back all the people.
I said, [?
Abba, ?] you know what?
I'm going to take them across the border.
Say, yeah?
OK.
You want to take 'em?
You're-- they're here.
Go ahead, take 'em
Sure enough, I tell the people-- you know, we used to work-- we used to make tricks, like-- we used to tell the people, like, listen.

When you get over on the other side and you get caught by the police, or by the border police, let's say, if you-- when we came, we took the people from Salzburg to [INAUDIBLE], which is Austria to Germany.
And they got caught in the German side, we used to tell them, I said, listen.
If you get caught, you tell that the reason you, you were going to smuggle the-- the border, you wanted to go from [INAUDIBLE] to Salzburg.
So you were going to smuggle the-- you were gonna cross the border to Austria.
They're gonna tell you no good, so you got to go back to Germany.
OK.
Then the people who we wanted to bring into Salzburg, we used to tell them, when you get caught, you tell them you wanted to go to Germany.
Say, oh, no good.
You can't go to Germany.
You gotta stay in Austria.
Good.
So, anyhow, what happened on this particular day-- it was on a Sunday afternoon-- and I'm the hero.
I want to bring them over to [INAUDIBLE].
Sure enough, we get a young group, about 50 people.
We come to [INAUDIBLE].
In [INAUDIBLE], the Army, the American army, the soldier, the border police, they were waiting already for us.
OK?
So, we-- we came in.
The train pulled into the station.
I told the people, I said, when we put into the station-- they had no luggage, nothing-- I says, you spread, you start running all different directions.
OK.
Sure enough, we came in there.
They surrounded us.
And we started [INAUDIBLE].
Now, for some reason, they knew me.
And they say, you the one who's there.
You under arrest.
OK.
They put me in a Jeep.
They locked me up.
They put me in jail in [INAUDIBLE].
OK.
Put me in [INAUDIBLE].
I was there for couple of days.
Same thing.
I told the guys in there, the guard, I said, go over to [INAUDIBLE], the camp.
And tell the guys that [INAUDIBLE] is arrested and that he is in this-- in this jail.
OK.
The meantime, [?
Abba ?] wanted to come and to liberate me.
[BACKROUND NOISE] So he's coming in in there.
And he's [? support. ?] He's going into the headquarters.
And he's telling them, he says, listen.
I'm the leader from-- we had, [INAUDIBLE] we had the-- we were called ourself, it was in-- the name of the group was CAGR.
Committee for Assistance to Jewish Refugees.
It was an organized group and it was legal.
Of course, whether it was legal, like, if they would tell us we could bring 50 people or 100 people, we would bring 1,000 people.
Because we used to go in, bring the people in there, and dump them in there, and take the papers, the documents-- that was my job-- then we go back to Salzburg and take another 50 people.
That's the way it worked.
So, [?
Abba's ?] coming in in there.
Sure enough, they lock him up.
OK.
Captain Novinsky finds out that both of us are in jail.
Now what is he gonna do?
So, we decide on a different day.
So me and [?
Abba, ?] we sitting in jail together.
We sitting there for about four or five days.
And like, maybe one of these-- [? well ?] [INAUDIBLE] they-- I was a big criminal.
They were-- I was told to-- they-- they were told to give me only one meal a day.
He's a very big criminal, very important criminal guy.
So, oh, OK.
So, Captain Novinsky decide what's he going to do?
He says, OK.
We gonna send Lieutenant [INAUDIBLE] to Chaplain [?
Kohn. ?] And they're gonna find out-- we were sitting in the-- the jail was the German jail.
Nothing ?] [INAUDIBLE] it was not an American jail.
So, Chaplain [?
Kohn, ?] Lieutenant [NON-ENGLISH], they came in.
And they had-- their command was the MP, you know?
But-- border police.
Constabulary, it was called.
The Jeep was painted with a C, Constabulary.
They comin' in.
And we knew when there was no army-- there was no army personnel, no Americans.
And these two men go in.
They say, we have an order to take out [INAUDIBLE] and [?
Abba Weinstein. ?] We have to bring them over to headquarters for interrogation.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Sure, sure, sure.
Sure.
[INAUDIBLE]
So in the meantime, I say, OK.
They have to fill out papers.
I say, OK, I'll fill out papers so they'll release me.
OK.
So [?
Abba's ?] telling me, he says, you know, you see what you can do.
Take all these papers what they have in there.
See if you could swipe them away so we'll have them.
Because--
I say, OK.
No problem.
So while he is talking in there, with all these thing, and they're trying to straighten things out for our release, I swipe all the papers, put them in my pocket.
I say [?
Abba, ?] we all set to go.
He say, OK.
So we went in, got in the Jeep, and we came back to Salzburg.
The Germans are still looking for us.
So that's-- that's was the trick of the trade.
I have a big question for you.
Yes?
Well, the money came-- the way we understood, it came from the Jewish agency.
And it came from the, also from the Joint, from the American Joint--
Yeah.
Yeah.
American Joint Distribution [?
Camp ?] it came.
And, you know, I mean, it wasn't extra [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, we used to-- I mean, the Americans and the-- all these people, they had to supply the-- the DP.
It was a DP.
Camp for displaced persons.
So they had to give us food.
They had to clothe us.
They had to give us food.
I mean, that was part of the immigration.
I mean, that was part of the displaced person.
They did it-- they didn't do it only for the Jews.
They did it for the Russians, too, or for the Polish, or for the Germans, a matter of fact.
That's why they pay.
I mean, our trucks, you know, we had 10 trucks and maybe with two, three sets of plates.
And we-- we-- every time, we used to take off-- there were two plates.
So, we took off one plate on one truck and one plate on the other truck.
So, it was OK.
We did-- the gasoline, we used to get from the army, in a depot.
We used to get all these information.
And we used to bring people from-- smuggle them-- bringing them over from Salzburg to [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE] We used to drive-- I used to drive a truck.
Matter of fact, the American soldiers used to laugh at me.
They used to say, you know, we were ready to shoot.
We saw a truck without a driver.
You know, you imagine me, a little guy like this, driving a 10-wheeler, you know, with-- with 50 or 100 people in it.
OK, it was [? done. ?]
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, there were women.
I mean, nothing-- [? well, ?] in 1946, I-- I met my wife.
Oh, that's another story.
Should I tell her the story?
I don't know.

Well, what happened-- you know, like I told you, I was in-- I was in charge and-- I mean, in-- to bringing in the transports and [? so on. ?] So what happened one time, there was a transport coming in from Vienna to Salzburg.
And these people, they were little trouble makers, what we call them.
They were from Betar, you know?
Of course, with Jews, there's always something.
There's-- there-- there-- there two Jews in 20 different parties, you know.
So, everybody, so, the Betar wanted to take over.
And then the-- we were-- we were-- not our-- the Berihah was supposed to be none-- no preferential parties.
We were for everybody.
So, these-- they were going to bring these people.
And we were supposed to pick 'em up on the railway station in Salzburg.
So, everybody was afraid to go.
I say, OK.
I'm not afraid to go.
I'll go.
I'll meet him in there.
So, OK.
Wear the uniform in there.
And I remember, [INAUDIBLE] we had a Austrian driver.
His name was [?
Hansel. ?]
And we had a big truck, like a huge [INAUDIBLE].
It was a-- a Czechoslovakian-- Tatra was the name of the truck.
It was like a cattle truck, you know.
So I pull up in there, for the railroad station.
And these big [? hills ?] are coming in there, and they're [? start ?] and this.
I says, listen, I said, don't start any trouble.
Because otherwise you're gonna sit in here.
I say, you go in to the truck.
We're gonna take you to Camp [?
Mill. ?] [?
At ?] that time, there was Camp [?
Mill. ?] I mean, we, in Salzburg, we had three, actually we had three camps.
There was Camp [?
Mill, ?] I, and Camp [?
Biyalik ?] So three different--
So, anyhow, they went up in the truck.
And I look on this little girl.
She was a cute little girl.
She caught my eye.
So, and these guys [? that ?] were [? giving ?] her, I says, OK.
So, I tell-- I saw her.
It was a real hot day.
Real hot.
So I tell the guy, I says, I tell to the driver, I says, listen.
Pull away from there.
It's too hot in there.
Pull up to the shade.
OK.
I pull over in the shade.
It was OK.
And then, finally, it quiet down.
They quiet down.
So I says, OK.
We'll take you to the camp.
So I took 'em there to the [?
Mill ?] Camp.
Came in [?
Mill ?] Camp.
We-- organized, we gave 'em lodging in there.
We gave 'em some food.
And this cute little girl was among them?
This cute little girl was among them.
So she comes up to me and she says, excuse me.
You with the Berihah?
I say, yeah.
Yeah.
She says, listen, you know, I'm having a problem.
I'm lost from my family, and I'm searching my family.
I can't find them.
Maybe you know how I could find them.
I say, yeah, later on, we-- we're gonna look.
We keep a list from all the people who went through.
I said, maybe we'll find them.
Yeah.
And that's it.
It started a little romance.
She fell in love with me.
[LAUGHS] And, you know, and we met.
And then--
And after a few-- got married in August '50-- '46.
And, thank god, we're married for 50 years with the same woman.
No complaints.
What, leave--
Well, that's another thing.
You know, I don't know.
I don't know.
I don't know.
It was-- what happened, to be honest with you, lately, when I was in Berihah from 1946-- actually from '45-- till 1950, it wasn't the same Berihah what I was in there.
And I got a little disappointed.
It was all kind of people came in, and new people.
And it was-- it was not the way it was supposed to be, what I meant to be.
So-- and besides, she-- I had an uncle in here.
And I had nobody.
I thought, I'll come to this country.
It'll be more time with the family, I'll find some family people.
And it didn't work out in there, so-- my wife wanted to be in here.
So I says, OK.
So I decided to come in here.
What do you mean, how?
The-- I came to the HIAS, to the immigration.
I had papers, my uncle send me an affidavit.
I never wanted to use it.
But then, later on, when I decided to, I wanted to-- to-- then I used the affidavit.
It's a matter of fact, when the-- when I decided to go to America, a lot of Israeli people were not too happy about it, the people I worked with.
Especially this guy, Mr.
[INAUDIBLE].
He later became-- he was a [INAUDIBLE] man in the Jewish agency.
And he liked me very much.
And one night, he came down.
He found out that we want to go for-- to the States.
And he sat down with us.
And he says to me, listen, [INAUDIBLE], he said, I'll tell you what.
He says, I don't care who goes, where they go.
But the way, what you did, you contribute so much to the Berihah.
He said, it's really a shame to lose you.
He said, I'll tell you what I'll offer you.
He says-- we already-- on that time, that's in 1948, when the state was born.
We already had legal immigration.
And we were already taking out people-- we were-- by planes.
And we had air flights from Salzburg to Tel Aviv.
He says, if you want to, he says, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Levine, ?] which was the general consul for Israel-- I knew him personally-- every time if I needed, I wanted his car to go to a [INAUDIBLE] or something, I used to take this car.
So he says, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Levine ?] will give you a visa.
He said, I'll give you a passport.
You could go to Israel.
He says, you stay in Israel.
I'll give you all the money you need.
And I guarantee you I'll settle you up-- you'll be-- I'll see to it that you get a good position.
And if you decide later on, you want to come back to the States to go [? to ?] America, he say, you free-- free to do so.
I say, no.
I don't want to do this.
I says, for some reason, I says, I decided I want to go to the States.
And he says, it's OK.
And I say, all right.
That's-- it's OK with me.
But in a way, when I met-- I went [INAUDIBLE] and then my friends, a lot of [? friends ?] come for me.
And all, or a lot of people from Berihah, who worked in Berihah, they always telling me, [INAUDIBLE], you would have been OK in Israel.
You would have a good position there because you were very famous.
He says, ?] you would have been doing OK in Israel, too.
OK, so--
February the 19, 1950.
I did all kind of work.
[INAUDIBLE] kind of work.
I started to work in a factory.
And then I-- yeah, mostly I did, like, a factory work and metal-- sheet metal, until I started my own little business.
I have, like, a little-- I had-- itwas pretty good.
I had a delivery service, which I started by myself.
And thank god, I can't complain.
I have a wonderful family.
Well, my wife, you met.
Not-- not only is she beautiful, she's also a very good woman.
The best one.
Yes.
I have two kids.
My son is 49 years old.
Yeah, my son was born in Austria, in Salzburg.
Then my daughter is, what, 42.
She's born in 1954 in the United States.
We have three grandchildren.
And I think we-- we OK.
Well, I would say it has an influence.
I mean, I always insisted that my children should never get married to non-Jew.
I mean, I am-- because I tell them what I went through, so much hell, only because I'm a Jew.
So I want them to know what it means to me.
So far, I'm lucky.
My daughter is married.
She's married to a nice-- as a matter of fact, she's married to one of my best friends' son.
We were-- we were like two brothers.
What I mentioned all the time, [INAUDIBLE] Safier.
That's one of the men that--
That's--
--you escaped from the camp with.
--the one-- we were-- yeah.
We-- we were born in the same town.
We lived together in the same town.
We escaped, we were hiding in the same town.
And he came in here, and unfortunately, died very young.
Died of cancer.
After going through all this Holocaust and all this thing.
He could have been well-off.
Unfortunately, he died.
So, I think-- my son, I hope still, maybe, some of these days, he'll get married.
And, if I ask you if you have any advice for your children or grandchildren or generations to come, for the future, what would you say?
Well, what could I say?
I hope they stay-- that they good people, they don't harm anybody, that they stay in the Jew-- in the Jewish faith.
And-- and to do the-- the best what they can.
You're very welcome.
